title: One-dimensional arrays

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C: Beginner to Intermediate draft for One-dimensional arrays; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: ISO C Working Group C23 status; ISO/IEC 9899:2024; provisions applicable to One-dimensional arrays
sources.claim: A C array is a contiguous fixed-size sequence of elements; array expressions often convert to pointers to the first element, but the array object and pointer are not the same type.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A C array is a contiguous fixed-size sequence of elements; array expressions often convert to pointers to the first element, but the array object and pointer are not the same type.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: An int values[4] has valid indexes zero through three, and sizeof values in its defining scope includes storage for all four elements. Your goal is to implement and test array traversal, update and aggregation routines. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: while, do and for; Parameters and return values. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: A C array is a contiguous fixed-size sequence of elements; array expressions often convert to pointers to the first element, but the array object and pointer are not the same type. This rule is applied to concrete C source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: No runtime bounds check is required, sizeof differs before and after parameter adjustment, and one-past pointers may be compared but not dereferenced. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: An array argument tells the called function its length automatically because the bracket size remains part of the parameter.

includes: Declare, initialize, measure in scope and traverse arrays within valid bounds.
Implement and test array traversal, update and aggregation routines.
excludes: No runtime bounds check is required, sizeof differs before and after parameter adjustment, and one-past pointers may be compared but not dereferenced.

outcomes: Implement and test array traversal, update and aggregation routines.

examples.title: One-dimensional arrays: defined case
examples.situation: An int values[4] has valid indexes zero through three, and sizeof values in its defining scope includes storage for all four elements.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: An int values[4] has valid indexes zero through three, and sizeof values in its defining scope includes storage for all four elements.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: Array parameters adjust to pointer parameters; pass the element count separately and enforce every index against that count.
examples.result: The defined result follows because Array parameters adjust to pointer parameters; pass the element count separately and enforce every index against that count.

examples.title: One-dimensional arrays: boundary case
examples.situation: When passed to a function parameter, values does not carry its element count, so a separate size_t count is supplied.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: When passed to a function parameter, values does not carry its element count, so a separate size_t count is supplied.
Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: No runtime bounds check is required, sizeof differs before and after parameter adjustment, and one-past pointers may be compared but not dereferenced.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: An array argument tells the called function its length automatically because the bracket size remains part of the parameter.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Implement and test array traversal, update and aggregation routines.

misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete C rules, explain why this belief fails: An array argument tells the called function its length automatically because the bracket size remains part of the parameter.

analogy.example: A manually managed workshop can model One-dimensional arrays: every tool, address, buffer, and acquired resource has an explicit valid range and owner.
usefulFor: Making low-level state, bounds, representation, ownership, and cleanup responsibilities concrete
limitations: C abstract-machine rules, undefined behavior, pointer provenance, translation, and implementation variation are stricter than a physical workshop analogy.
No runtime bounds check is required, sizeof differs before and after parameter adjustment, and one-past pointers may be compared but not dereferenced.
